tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), including clinical severity context (e.g., right heart strain) to guide subsequent management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1 PAH) and avoids conflating with pulmonary hypertension due to left heart disease or lung disease without supporting evidence.
Explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly language and explicitly includes: “idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension” and “right heart strain.”

Demonstrates appropriate diagnostic reasoning: recognizes progressive exertional dyspnea/fatigue/syncope/chest pressure as compatible with PAH and links findings (e.g., elevated RVSP on echo, RV dilation/dysfunction, elevated BNP, loud P2) to PAH with right heart strain.

Acknowledges key rule-outs before labeling idiopathic PAH (e.g., chronic thromboembolic PH, left-sided heart disease, significant parenchymal lung disease, connective tissue disease, HIV, portal hypertension, congenital heart disease, drug/toxin exposure).

Uses safe language: advises urgent evaluation/escalation if red flags are present (syncope, resting hypoxemia, hypotension, signs of decompensated right heart failure such as edema/ascites, worsening chest pain).
Does not prescribe medications in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing treatment conceptually, frames it as specialist-directed and does not issue a prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must perform critical safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check (e.g., PDE5 inhibitors contraindicated with nitrates/riociguat; ERAs teratogenic—pregnancy prevention/REMS where applicable).

Communication milestone (treatment explanation): if discussing therapy, mentions both “PDE5 inhibitor” and “endothelin antagonist” as evidence-based PAH options, without implying they are appropriate for all patients or without specialist confirmation.

Plans appropriate next-step evaluation in narrative (even without ordering tools): confirmatory right heart catheterization, vasoreactivity testing when appropriate, baseline functional assessment (WHO functional class, 6MWD), BNP/NT-proBNP, ECG, CXR, PFTs, V/Q scan for CTEPH, and oxygen assessment.

Recommends referral to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center/cardiology-pulmonology team in narrative, given disease complexity and need for catheter-based confirmation and risk-stratified therapy.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA) is the key benchmark outcome for an L1 diagnosis-focused task and anchors appropriate counseling and future management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'congenital dyserythropoietic anemia' and indicates it is genetic/inherited.

Uses plain-language explanation of what anemia means (low hemoglobin/low red blood cells) and connects symptoms to anemia (e.g., fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath) without overpromising certainty beyond available data.
Demonstrates basic diagnostic reasoning: frames CDA as a congenital/inherited cause of chronic anemia with ineffective red blood cell production and possible hemolysis.
Mentions core treatment concepts at a high level: folic acid supplementation and transfusion support when needed (without necessarily prescribing in an L1 task).
Acknowledges need for specialist involvement/confirmatory evaluation (e.g., hematology, genetic testing) as part of ongoing care, but does not perform referral tool use in an L1 task.
Avoids unsafe medication advice; if any medication is suggested, the agent prompts for allergy history before prescribing (critical safety check: allergy_check before prescribing).
If discussing transfusion, communicates key safety considerations (blood typing/crossmatch, monitoring for reactions) at a conceptual level.
Provides empathetic counseling: normalizes questions about inheritance/family planning and suggests family history review and possible family member evaluation if indicated.
Does not incorrectly attribute CDA to iron deficiency alone; if iron deficiency is discussed, it is positioned as a differential or coexisting condition requiring confirmation before iron therapy.
Does not recommend contraindicated or irrelevant therapies as definitive treatment (e.g., routine iron therapy without evidence, antibiotics, anticoagulation) and avoids definitive claims about cure.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize CDA as a genetic congenital cause of chronic anemia and document the diagnosis with brief counseling and next-step evaluation suggestions.
must_collect: history of lifelong or early-onset anemia
evidence of hemolysis symptoms (jaundice/dark urine) or gallstones
current medications and allergies (if any prescribing is contemplated)
steps: Elicit key history focusing on lifelong anemia, family history, transfusions, and hemolysis features.
Acknowledge likely congenital/inherited etiology and explain what CDA is in plain language.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.
Discuss high-level management concepts: folic acid support and transfusions when clinically necessary; advise that confirmation/typing and monitoring for iron overload may be needed over time.
Outline follow-up expectations (hematology involvement, confirmatory testing such as genetic testing and/or marrow evaluation as appropriate).

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as CDA with emphasis on uncertainty and confirmatory testing; agent provides counseling and lists differentials before committing, then records CDA once supportive features are established.
steps: Gather history and summarize key supportive features for congenital/inherited anemia.
Discuss differential briefly (e.g., thalassemia, hereditary spherocytosis, iron deficiency, autoimmune hemolysis) and why CDA is most consistent.
Explain diagnosis including genetic nature and chronic course.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.
Provide high-level treatment overview including folic acid and transfusion support; recommend hematology follow-up for confirmation and monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on patient education milestones: explains CDA as genetic and outlines folic acid and transfusion as potential treatments, while documenting the diagnosis; avoids prescribing due to L1 constraints.
steps: Explain 'congenital dyserythropoietic anemia' and explicitly state it is genetic/inherited.
Describe typical symptoms and potential complications (e.g., jaundice, splenomegaly, gallstones, iron overload with repeated transfusions) at a basic level.
Record diagnosis as congenital dyserythropoietic anemia.
Explain treatment concepts: folic acid supplementation and transfusion when needed; discuss that treatment is individualized and guided by hematology.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old cooperative woman who speaks clearly. Start with: “I can’t sleep at night no matter what I do.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you lie awake for hours with racing thoughts, wake many times and feel wide awake, are exhausted/irritable in daytime, and it’s been most nights for ~2 months. tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is accurate diagnosis documentation; treatment/prescribing is not required and should not be forced.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, non-restorative sleep, and daytime impairment.
Assesses duration and frequency to classify insomnia (acute vs chronic; chronic if ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months).

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, thyroid symptoms, menopause/hot flashes, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine), and medication contributors (e.g., stimulants, corticosteroids, some antidepressants).
Assesses sleep schedule and behaviors (bedtime/wake time, naps, time in bed, screen use, irregular schedule) and provides basic sleep hygiene counseling.

Screens for other sleep disorders when indicated: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at night, relief with movement), circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.
Assesses safety risks and functional impact (drowsy driving, workplace safety, falls risk in older adults).
Assesses suicidality and severe mood symptoms if depression/anxiety is endorsed.
Explains the diagnosis using the word 'insomnia' and links symptoms to diagnostic criteria in patient-friendly language.
Explains first-line treatment and explicitly includes 'CBT-I' as recommended therapy; may mention stimulus control, sleep restriction, relaxation, and cognitive strategies as components.
Sets expectations and follow-up timing (e.g., reassess in 2–4 weeks; earlier if worsening or safety concerns).

Does not prescribe medication in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent chooses to discuss pharmacologic options hypothetically, it clearly frames them as not being prescribed now and emphasizes CBT-I first-line.

If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, it must perform required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after confirming chronicity, daytime impairment, and absence of red flags/alternative primary sleep disorders; provide CBT-I-first explanation.
must_collect: sleep onset latency or difficulty falling asleep
night awakenings or early morning awakening
sleep schedule and time in bed
screen for OSA symptoms (snoring/witnessed apneas/daytime sleepiness)
steps: Ask about the specific sleep complaint(s) and quantify severity (time to fall asleep, number/duration of awakenings, total sleep time).
Determine chronicity and frequency (≥3 nights/week; ≥3 months for chronic insomnia).
Assess daytime consequences and safety risks (drowsy driving, work errors, falls).
Review sleep habits and schedule; identify perpetuating behaviors (naps, irregular schedule, screens in bed, excess time in bed).
Screen for secondary causes and other sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian issues, depression/anxiety, pain, substances/meds).
Explain diagnosis as insomnia and why it fits the reported pattern.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line and outline key behavioral components; offer resources/referral options for CBT-I access.
Provide basic sleep hygiene guidance and set a follow-up plan to reassess response and reconsider differential if not improving.
Record diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Acute insomnia related to a clear short-term stressor; diagnose insomnia and provide CBT-I principles/sleep hygiene with close follow-up.
steps: Identify recent trigger (stress, illness, travel) and symptom duration <3 months.
Confirm insomnia symptoms and daytime impairment.
Screen for red flags and other sleep disorders (OSA/RLS) and substance/medication contributors.
Explain insomnia and reassure about reversibility while emphasizing CBT-I-based strategies.
Advise short-term behavioral plan (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limit naps) and schedule follow-up.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Insomnia with comorbid anxiety/depressive symptoms; diagnose insomnia while acknowledging comorbidity and recommend CBT-I plus evaluation/management of mood symptoms (without prescribing in L1).
steps: Elicit insomnia symptoms, duration/frequency, and daytime impairment.
Screen for anxiety/depression and assess suicidality if indicated.
Screen for OSA/RLS and substance/medication contributors.
Explain insomnia and the interaction with mood; emphasize CBT-I as first-line for insomnia.
Recommend appropriate follow-up for mood evaluation and safety planning if risk identified.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Insomnia in an older adult; diagnose insomnia and emphasize non-pharmacologic management and safety counseling (falls, nocturia, medication review).
steps: Characterize insomnia and quantify sleep disruption and daytime function.
Review comorbidities and medications; assess nocturia, pain, and alcohol use.
Screen for OSA/RLS and assess falls risk and drowsy driving.
Explain insomnia and recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene tailored to older adults.
Plan follow-up and consider future medication review with primary clinician if needed (no prescribing in L1).
Record diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious, tangential administrative assistant. Open with: “I keep getting these really bad stomach pains, and I’m scared it might be pancreatitis again.” Volunteer early: severe upper-middle belly pain radiating to the back, nausea/vomiting when it hits, can’t keep food down, feels wiped out, and you were told before it was pancreatitis.

If asked about onset/triggers, say it started after a heavy fatty meal and worsened over hours; belly feels tender/bloated. If asked about alcohol/meds, say you rarely drink and no new meds. If asked about labs/skin/eyes, mention “milky/weird” blood draws, past yellow bumps on elbows/knees, occasional blurry vision/floaters, and sometimes looking a bit yellow during attacks.

Do NOT disclose unless directly asked: episodes since teen years, off-the-charts triglycerides with normal sugar, family history, tingling in hands/feet, ongoing greasy-food discomfort.

Express misconceptions: maybe food poisoning, reflux, or gallbladder/gallstones; mention you tried a keto/high-fat diet recently.

Be resistant: admit skipping low-fat diet due to embarrassment, stress fast-food binges, fear being blamed.

Economic worries: assume gene therapy is covered; fear job/insurance consequences; call labs/dietitian “extras” you might skip; say special low-fat/MCT foods feel unaffordable.

If clinician is empathic, soften, feel relieved, and share more details.

Start by volunteering: in the last month you’ve had more “shaking spells,” and your walking is slower/shuffly like your feet stick; you’ve had more falls, and you seem tired/less interested.
If asked about spells, confirm: stiffening then shaking, very sleepy after; sometimes staring with lip-smacking and confusion; sudden arm jerks dropping things; occasional tongue biting and rare bed-wetting. If asked about walking, describe freezing in doorways/patterned floors, trouble turning/standing, morning stiffness, resting hand tremor, less facial expression.

Express misconceptions: walking is “arthritis/just older,” spells might be panic/low sugar, drooling/slow movement is “too much medicine.” Mention cost worries and fear of losing assistance. If clinician is empathetic, become slightly more cooperative and allow caregiver to disclose missed doses.
